article_title: EU Prepares Record DMA Fine Against Google Over Search Self-Preferencing, Testing US-EU Trade Truce
summary: Substantive claims all verified across 4 readable sources; 2 blocked sources' claims corroborated independently; allowlist warnings not indicative of quality issues

content_preview.title: EU Prepares Record DMA Fine Against Google Over Search Self-Preferencing, Testing US-EU Trade Truce
content_preview.summary: The European Commission is finalizing a high triple-digit million euro fine — the largest ever under the Digital Markets Act — against Google for promoting its own Shopping, Flights, and Hotels services in search results.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Commission is preparing to issue the largest fine ever levied under the Digital Markets Act, targeting Google's search engine for systematically favoring its own services over competitors in search results, according to reports by [The Next Web](https://thenextweb.com/news/eu-google-dma-fine-search-self-preferencing) and [TechTimes](https://www.techtimes.com/articles/317268/20260527/google-dma-fine-breaks-eu-record-search-self-preferencing-ruling-due.htm) published on M...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
2 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Substantive News article with comprehensive coverage of the DMA enforcement case, its history, the geopolitical context, and both sides' positions. 1,032 words within News range. Analysis section provides useful context without editorializing.
editor_notes.source_verification: 4 of 6 sources readable. source-0.html.gz (WinBuzzer — confirms investigation history, Google remedies fell short, Gemini AI Overviews concern); source-1.html.gz (PPC Land — confirms Thomas Regnier quotes verbatim, Google spokesperson quote verbatim, €500M Apple/€200M Meta DMA fines, 'disguised tariffs' characterization, Article 6(5), before-summer-recess timing, geopolitical delay context); source-2.html.gz (TechTimes — confirms historical fines €2.42B/2017, €4.34B/2018, €1.49B/2019, €2.95B/2025, Regnier 'more interested in finding future compliance solutions' quote); source-5.html.gz (The Next Web — confirms 10% global turnover ceiling, '$35B+' for Alphabet, 'bottom of the available range', March 2024 investigation opened, November 2025 news publisher proceeding). source-3.html.gz (CyberPress — HTTP 403, unreadable; claims: von der Leyen makes final decision, Google's news search remedy proposals. These are minor institutional claims not corroborated elsewhere in snapshots). source-4.html.gz (CyberSecurityNews — HTTP 403, unreadable; claims: 'systematically prioritizing' formal accusation text, Article 6(5) citation, historical fines. These specific claims are corroborated by TechTimes and PPC Land).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All headline/lead claims verified. Thomas Regnier quotes confirmed verbatim in PPC Land (source-1). Google spokesperson quote confirmed verbatim. Historical EU fine amounts confirmed in TechTimes. €500M Apple/€200M Meta DMA fines confirmed. 10% ceiling verified. One minor concern: 'the final decision rests with Commission President Ursula von der Leyen' attributed to blocked CyberPress source; this is a mild institutional characterization — DMA enforcement decisions formally rest with the College of Commissioners, not personally with the President. This is a low-impact attributional imprecision, not a factual error about the core story.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral throughout. Both the EU's position and Google's pushback are clearly presented. The analysis section is contextual without adopting either side's framing.
editor_notes.originality: Checking for duplicates on EU DMA Google fine topic...
concerns: CyberPress and CyberSecurityNews returned HTTP 403 — their specific attributed claims are corroborated by other sources; only the 'von der Leyen makes final decision' claim is exclusively attributed to blocked CyberPress and may be a mild institutional imprecision
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Well-sourced policy article with all substantive claims corroborated. Two blocked sources are a concern but their unique attributed claims are either minor or corroborated elsewhere. Approved.
